So thank you everybody for that. I can't tell you how much it meant to me, right? You know when someone that you really love passes away, it's the weirdest thing you're conflicted You know you're wanting to go in the end because they're in pain, but then it give a life without them Pretty tough right anyway But I did use the time to really develop I think it's an extra 45 minutes of content of new ideas and strategies For developing a massive shiver of furls and also for upselling cross-selling I really just you know I had time I just said I make the most of it I just dug into that so this module you're about to get tomorrow is gonna blow you away One of the things that Occurred to me, you know when I did some research is that this is really weird You know notion out there. It's online right about when is the best time to ask for referrals? And the collective wisdom out there among the so-called sales traders, right? I call that right? We'll know what they're about most of them right pretty much all of them Um They say you have to wait the problem the mistake that people make is they ask for referrals Too quickly too early in the game And because of that they blow the whole thing up And I just gotta tell you something guys. That's just nonsensical It's crap. It's not true And I I started scratching my head saying why is this out there? Listen, I've sold everything I haven't sold a financial price household goods, you know food entertainment tangible intangible online. It's like You know, you know, I asked referrals Right as soon as I close someone I asked them immediately and I start saying them and I get them I get more referrals of anyone I know in fact when I was in the mortgage business, I got so many referrals. It was like it was legendary How many I get from like one person, right? And there's a reason why and I'll tell you what it is See when you're using the straight line what happens when I close I don't close someone by the skin of my teeth It's not like they're at a low level of certainty and I just impulse them into a sale You get it. In other words, when I close someone I've gotten them to such a high level of certainty both logically and emotionally and I've also positioned myself from the very start as this world-class expert high level expert and because of that I've essentially earned the right or put it another way. They feel comfortable enough To give me referrals right away I don't have to wait for some arbitrary amount of time to pass until they've seen all the benefits experienced them They've you know, got the whole thing under their belts. They just know for sure, right? They're so certain When I close That they run to give me referrals and I'm not saying this to impress you about my ability. It's something to do with that This is about you understanding that now that you're really closing and you know, you're perfecting the straight line system You Close people not in the traditional way where they're just impulsive that barely close You really got a solid person on your hands and that person Is going to give you a furrow straight away Now not every single time But I would say you know 50 60 percent of the time you ask so for a furrow. I'll tell you this way. How about this? 95 percent of the time someone who's ultimately going to give you a furrow will give it to you right away Some people just don't give referrals out, right? But this notion that you have to wait For three months or six months that is not going to work. And let me just tell you what ends up happening In the same way that you have like, you know in love between men and women you have the friend zone And you got the love zone, right? Either your friends or your intimate, right? Well, when you spend too much time in the friend zone You never can get back into the love zone. You're an established friend zone person, right? Well, when it comes to referrals you have the referral zone And you're the non referral zone You're one of those books that they don't give referrals to or they do give referrals to if you spend too much time with someone And you're a been there provider of a service or a good and you haven't gotten referrals You're never going to get any you get into that zone of zone. They just don't give referrals to I Put myself I insert myself into the referral zone Right out of the start now. Obviously there's some strategy to that, right? And you know, I dive into this so intimately in certification. It's such a game You know why because some of the facts that I uncovered here were that number one Listen to this crazy dichotomy They did some polling on this. They had a big study Only 29 of people who buy various goods and services End up giving referrals 29 Yet when they pulled that same group 90 percent of them said they would They were just never asked what asked properly What an opportunity for an untapped market. In other words, 90 percent are saying they would give referrals But only 29 percent actually do Now for those of you with terrible at math, I'll do it for you. It's 61 percent There you have this huge untapped Market is untapped source Of new people to essentially call it already will be very high On the certainty scale for certainly the second 10, right? You the salesperson and by default the first 10 as well and the third 10 And yet that's what referrals do they they they essentially like put you in a race imagine like a hundred yard dash And you start at a 95 yard line. That's what getting your referral is So when you get referrals, they bring down your elite costs They dramatically increase your closing rate because plain and simple Referrals are easy to close because they enter in higher on the certainty scale So what it does is it brings down the overall operating costs of your business So if you own a business And you're not using the straight line system Not only is your closing rate going to be lower, but you don't close the sort of sales That and end up turning into these long-term Customers life or raving fans who buy from you again again or buy from your company again and ultimately give your salespeople massive referrals What a lost opportunity. They don't they don't fit me so right So we after about 15 20 minutes. I It's one of the rules I teach about sending them to your competitor and I always make up You know, I always tell the story about you know mirror the movie miracle on 34th street, right? You know how there's the macy's santum and you know, he's the real santa, of course in the movie, right? And he Refers some woman to gimbals and back in the 30s or 40s, whatever it was, right? And because gimbals was macy's competitor and the woman comes in to buy ice case for a kid They're under the right type of ice case and the santa claus sends the woman to the competitor And of course the upper level executives find out and they want to fire the santa claus But it turns out that the newspapers get a hold of so they turn it into this massive pr storm, right? And mr. Macy himself realizes the value of this And of course then mr. Gimbal finds out wait a second macy's is doing I want to send my customers to macy's and then mr. Macy and mr. Gimbal They shake hands. That's all right. Howl in a spirit, right? But the point is is that when you're on the receiving end of it It is profound. Let me tell you something Brioni could not have sold me a suit that day. They didn't have it in stock, right? My next clothing purchase Is gonna be from brioni I feel indebted to them. I feel that I owe them something. That's reciprocity and they have beautiful stuff Right there famous, you know for having the greatest suits, right? You know, I'm gonna make it a point Now I'm not gonna run there with a marker But next time I'm walking down madison avenue or or rodeo drive if I pass by a brioni So you're damn sure I'm gonna go in there and I'm gonna try to buy something because of the level of customer service That they gave me Now that in this case, you know, I've been a customer. So I guess I'm already a customer of brioni I've put a lot of their clothes in the past, right? I never a suit. So I'm already a customer and they refer me out So like that's what we call Creating a customer for life. That's one of the rules I teach It's so powerful. Why well number one, obviously there's the reciprocity, right? They do this for you. I'm like, wow I'm just blown away, but also I know that in the future if I need some type of clothing and you know, maybe there's a brioni nearby If I walk into brioni, if that's their policy, that's how they treat their good customers And I know even if they don't have what I need Guess what the sales person will send me to where I got to go So I'm always going to look at brioni now as a first point of contact It's so powerful And I'm glad that I get to endorse brioni right now. All right, because of what they did they just treated me like gold They send me to that competitor Now, obviously, let me say this. There are certain industries where this just would not make any sense, right? So if you're in a business, we have like one product and it's like, you know, if they were to use your competitor They'll never use you again. Obviously, you don't want to send that customer to that competitor But that's more the exception to the rule for the most part you can send people to your competitor And in nurse such good will as a result of that That you truly then have a customer for life that will give you referrals I'm gonna refer people to brioni like I'm doing it right now. I I feel I just think I feel I feel warm and fuzzy about them And they got beautiful clothes and everyone knows that that was just my own experience. So when I Teach this whole notion of creating customers for life. I have this set of Guidelines of protocols. There are six or seven of them in all, right? And let me give you a couple of them right now And then I want to move into some other aspects of you know, what you do after you close the sale how do you take this one-time buyer and ensure that they do become a long-term customer that Buys again and again and gives you referrals, right? So one of the main things here one of the overarching rules ready for Don't ever duck a phone call Or an email or a text in other words If there's a problem you sell something no matter how hard you try there's going to be problems, right? If a customer is trying to solve a problem and you make yourself scarce you duck a phone call in the olden days That's what used to be very easy to do now. It's the texting is not quite as easy, but someone texts you don't respond You just make yourself scarce You will never fully have this customer's trust again Can the relationship be sort of prepared? Yeah, somewhat but never fully The most toxic thing that you can do Is duck someone's call when there's a problem? Here's the deal we are conditioned We've been essentially desensitized to expect and accept bad news We know that when we buy things they don't always go as planned. We know things are going to break they're not going to function well But the question is what do you do when that happens? What do you do when you're on the ass end of that situation? You know you sell something and whatever and it just is a problem Do you be the person that puts your best foot forward and helps that customer resolve that problem? Or do you hide behind your telephone or your your smartphone with texting or what's up? You don't answer you make yourself scarce, right? Let me tell you something Stratton with all the mistakes I made with ethics and integrity back then with customer service One thing I never allowed. We did not allow our salespeople to duck phone calls never Not only is it the death knell of any long-term business But you're only begging for someone to sue you what at least to complain and nowadays with the internet come on your reputation Is everything it's really hard to repair So you got to get out in front and here's the irony by the way is that once there's a problem And you actually resolve it It's an opportunity to build even more trust Think about when you're on the receiving end of that. What happens when you buy something? Think back and it didn't actually function or work the way it was supposed to the problem Something went wrong and the salesperson who sold a few stepped sold you stepped in Went above and beyond to make sure that your problem got sorted out Rather than just passing it off to a customer service person or simply being scarce, which is the worst thing, right? What did you feel inside? About the salesperson. How did it impact your perception? Did you want to buy from this person again? Did you what did you resent them for even having a problem in the front? No You wouldn't resent to say oh man. I didn't problem. No, you'd say wow This company they stand behind their word What are the great opportunities to build trust? To develop a long-term customer for life is precisely that When problems arise you want to handle them immediately and don't just pass them off to someone else Don't say oh, yeah go to my yeah, you know go to the customer success or customer service. Don't do that Get be a mention as they say right get on the phone as much as at least at least Put transfer the person be involved in the process even if you can't fix it yourself Keep your finger in the process. I promise you the level of trust and appreciation That that brings into the relationship Is priceless? Truly. Okay now next big one We always talk about this obvious fact that you want to this is an obvious one, right? But i'm going to take it a different way you want to Under promise and over deliver now that's so obvious that it's almost a cliche Everyone says it Very few people actually do it, but it's a cliche We all know that you're supposed to under promise and over deliver right, you know We call this the management of expectation The worst thing you could do is you know is you'll make all these wild promises to someone and then you deliver so much like you're all go above and beyond but Even you're above and beyond is not living up to the outrageous promises you made and they hate you This is not about the promise and what you deliver it's about the management of expectation Like it's like an arbitrary thing. What? Does the product do what is it? What benefits does it deliver what features does it have right? You need to not go on and on. So here's where I want to dig in. This is the difference Why does it happen if we all know how toxic it is? To over promise and i'm gonna why there's so many salespeople do it. You know why? Because they don't Write out or don't even know about looping patterns What happens is as the sales starts to drag on and they run out of things to say they start making things up They start exaggerating making claims and building and building on the on there on the benefits until they've ended up in a spot That's outrageous. It's not grounded in reality so now even if the person closes and they have let's say product works exactly as it's been designed to work But you said things in the process of closing and you're looping That made this thing seem like it was like the next you know, it was like the messiah itself. You're done I don't care how great the benefits were if you're all promised You're you're not gonna have a good end here. This is about the management of expectation So what's the solution? Well, what I want you to do Is I want you to really take a good look at all of the looping patterns that you've developed For each of the three tens remember you have a primary secondary tertiary. You know the fourth one as well, right? make sure that In the heat of the moment when you were right and everybody mentions I want to go to a reality check here make sure That in the process of closing You don't say things that put you into that zone where you can't possibly deliver It's the stupidest thing you could do and I'll tell you 99 percent of the time It's simply because the salesman didn't take the time to prepare They didn't have enough intelligent things scripted out to say and because of that they said something that was outrageous When by the way, they could have said something that was totally in line with what they could deliver on And they blow it What a shame The management of expectation is everything So please please please I want you to as a reality check here And this is by the way in the the uh certification train We go back now in this exercise portion I take you through all of that and we have detailed examples and show you how to act If not, if you're not on that you gotta go back and check these patterns It's the death knell Of a long-term relationship and also you're gonna get cancellations. All right, so be mindful of that All right next Here's an interesting one about giving gifts There are there is a couple of places where you and I'm talking about not Major gifts. I'm talking about ethical gifts. They could be as small as a thank you know It could be a coffee table book with pictures of you know famous locations or travel destinations or food or restaurants depending on what We call the prospect or your new customers aliveness meaning what makes them feel alive outside of work Put it this way the worst gift You can give a plumber is what a wrench You don't give gifts to people that are Essentially things that they would use in the workplace, you know, you're not gonna give a section. Oh, he has a free stapler That's not a gift that's gonna ennour any good will towards you. You want to find out what their Interests are outside of work now. You say wait a second, Jordan. I thought that that's going off the Pluto. Yes, it is when you're in that Pre-closing phase. So when you're gathering intelligence before you've closed someone Absolutely, you don't want to be spending all this time on their personal life That's the telltale son of a novice and it does not bill report actually repels people but Once you close someone Guess what the rules of the game change Now you actually want to go off the blue. It's not pluto anymore. Pluto actually comes inside the boundaries And questions about their personal life interests birthdays and adversaries. What are their children do? How old are they? You know, do they go to college? Finding out about what someone's life is like outside of work is crucial Because then when you give gifts to people You want to meet those needs tailor your gifts to match what people feel Alive about outside of work. Let me give you another great example here back in the day, right? When I had stratton we had this massive phone bill so every single phone company and was in the early days of deregulation, right? They were all after me for my half a million dollar a month phone bill, right? One day company comes along and says oh they and I actually had them they were providing with some line some service, right? They gave me tickets to like I was like a playoff game for baseball the Mets or something or the Yankees And you know what? I hate baseball I couldn't care less. I almost felt offended like they were trying to bribe me Seriously, I felt like okay. Well, they didn't be tickets. I wonder what's wrong with their products Seriously, I really was not impressed that was turned off yet Now I know Roger Federer and the dollar link probably eight or nine years old at the time, right? But if they would have given me tickets to the us open when I wimbled in or to augusta forgot man They would have had my business The person made the fatal mistake of not finding out what I liked Guys, this is so profound. It's so easy to do to not it's so easy to follow. Let's not make this mistake But before you go out giving gifts to people Don't invite people to sporting events or into sky boxes if they don't like what you're offering I still get it all a lot of people inviting me out to places that I don't care about And no interest at all tells me is they're trying to bribe me their product is probably subpar because they need to bribe me versus If someone takes the time to find out what I really like and then fills that need That's how you develop a customer for life. That's how you develop reciprocity And it builds massive trust massive rapport And sets you up for greatness. So be very very careful about that. Okay now Let's get a bit. Let me switch hats now about customers for life, right? But um, let's talk about What happens after you close the sale? What are the different phases that we have here and how do we essentially maximize each one? You loop back, right and boom you close the deal It's making up, you know very quick. I know I'm not a great artist, right? All right. So you close the deal right here, okay? Well, guess what? It's not over. So your clothes is right here Now you have what's called your onboarding and adoption phases You can't just get up right there. You have to onboard people correctly What you'll find very often is if you don't have any sort of onboarding program in place People won't even know how to use your product correctly. Sometimes they won't even use it They'll buy it. What are the processes that you use? They know How important it is that when someone buys from you someone becomes part of the john Maxwell team Well, the reason why there's such a high success rate the people that actually entered their program Is because they don't leave it up to chance. They have this massive support system That holds new people's hands every step of the way and ensures that they get on boarded correctly They do a part because they're ethical part because it's great business But at the end of the day what it leads to is people having a great customer experience Staying with the program not canceling and also referring their friends Yeah, I have to onboard correctly if you leave this up to chance You're really setting yourself up to have a very difficult time of it. I promise you that onboarding is crucial And then the next phase is called adoption And what that means now it differs from onboarding in the sense that onboarding is at the early stage People get basic familiarity to practice start using it Adoption means when it's us to get rolled out to the entire company to beat a V situation Or when someone has to lean on the start using all the various and the deeper features secondary features and benefits In other words, they really get in and start using it that's maximum potential across the board You must Not End the sale here the sale begins here now you got to onboard adoption And then it's in this adoption phase and by the way right in your onboarding right after your onboarding in your adoption where you start to engage in upsells for ourselves And you ask for referrals right here in your onboarding Okay, and I would get to that and it said probably name it. I'll do that next week or the week after okay But the bottom line here is that please don't be one of these people Who spends all this time? Perfecting the straight line in terms of closing it But you're full short right here And you don't make sure that people have a great experience And they start actually not just using it a little bit but use it in earnest Use the whole prog and get the most out of that Ethical it's great business and at least massive referrals and people that buy from you again and again now Before I open this up for questions here, how long we have how long has it been so far? Now 40 30. Yeah 36 minutes. I want to go through a little major point here. Okay, and In terms of referrals on the hand of referrals fully in a separate training here, right? But I want to just I want to give you some food for thought before I open this up the questions I wanted a lot of q&a today, right? Um, so I'll go as long as you want. I just keep asking me to ask me questions, right? Um, when it comes to referrals The key here is language How you ask them you never want to say oh, can you do it? You can can you give me some referrals? Do you also when you can refer to me the word referral has a lot of you know, not really rightfully so but that Is some negative baggage attached as money being made possibly kick. It's just like it's not the right word to use So we don't call referrals. It says you do anyone who I can help In the same way that I helped you And it's about helping someone You know anybody who I can you help it has the same problem or a simple problem I could help in the same way I helped you and now that's sort of phase one Someone that you're helping with your service, right? Your product solving a problem The second part is the most important one of all There's two types of ways to ask someone a question and get a response One way is called fill in the blank Fill in the blank means do you know anyone who you who I could help in the same way that I helped you Fill in that's a fill in the blank Far more effective is to use what's called multiple choice meaning Give them prompts any friends any family any neighbors any business associates Anyone you can think of you give them the answers that you're looking for those tell them Exactly a what criteria you're looking for If you do that it makes people feel much more comfortable to our folks They know they want to race in your time or the person's time people are scared of it They don't know if I like that person can be right. What if they hope it's just a waste of time They don't want to waste their friend's time So the more specific you know anyone that could help that maybe is you know I'm behind on their credit cards or someone that is having the same problems You would have yourself so many things out there, right? But the point is in any friends any neighbors in the area any co-workers any of your businesses Anyone you can think of at all You play multiple choice. I I promise you this Test it out You'll get 500 percent. I'm serious more referrals by telling them who you want Specifically what type of people and then giving them prompts friends family neighbors business associates Other people it's a beat-a-beasing other people that are in the business like you're a civil business anyone you could think of Unless they oh, yeah, you're jogging their memory It's so much more effective than just saying you know anybody who I could help Suffering for the same problem. You know, whatever it might be, right? I could help in the same way I helped you You're gonna get so much of a better response by playing the multiple choice game Right and use word I can help Okay, any like serve in the same way Language is powerful If you use the wrong words, I used to get referrals right now one more point about referrals before we opens up to questions and answers And how about a whoop? Okay, so um, here's the deal Like anything else We always work backwards the straight line is a backwards looking system What I mean by that is that if I want to Build the referral business. Well the first question I asked myself And I'm by the way, I live this as a salesperson. I always do how many referrals do I want to get each month? What's my target? You have to set a target Without that you have nothing to aim for it always starts with having a target to aim for a goal That's my goal. How many referrals do I want to get each month? Now I'll give you a perfect example when I was in the mortgage business, right? I used to go out into the field. We also did cold calling. We had marketing as well, but I Personally, I love going out door to door, right? I love going business to business and I was making so much money Going business to business and say hey, um, you know, it's the owner around they'd say hey, I'm hey Hey, my name's Jordan. I'm mortgage broker. What rate you paying right now on your on your home That was as simple as that no magic words and they say I'm paying eight and a half. I go really Oh, I can definitely save you a ton of money. I take out my book, right and start doing my thing right well Here's the deal I was going to stores for the most part that were like Neighborhood seafood store the owner's there, right? I go into hassle ons I go into any business you could imagine You know one of those a mailbox stores, right anything in every restaurant didn't matter. I went to a jewelry store You name it with auto body shops every single one But also I was keenly aware that there were certain types of people That could give me massive referrals for instance accountants Tax planners tax services real estate agents brokers, right? um Lawyers estuary, right? So I made it a point I said every single day No matter how many doors I've hit no matter how many people I've closed I am going to make it a point to approach at least 10 lawyers slash accountants slash I had 10 a day I would hit 10 if I had a drive out of my way just to hit 10 of these every single day I did it. I would not go home until I did and I ended up hitting people that became such Massive referrals of business one example was a place called it was called far and tax nice people in the world out in the Lounge, right? And I walked in there. They were incredibly hostile to me, right? You know I walked in the whole thing and then within five minutes, you know She's like, I'll give you one right because I did my thing with the straight line, right? These people became my best source. They probably gave me 150 referrals in one year And I made an average of eight to 10,000 in each one. It's over a million dollars one door Every single day I made it a point to knock on at least 10 doors that were in the referral zone Even if I had to do seven o'clock at night. I'm out there. I'm just I'm not going home So it starts with setting a target to aim for and then working backwards. How many do you have to hit each day? Why was it 10? I had a one in 10 shot I want to get one refer every single day and that's how I do my business. I go through cold calling through referrals And by closing at the highest level All right now I got the big bobo here All right, let's bring bo in here bones in the yoke with my dad.
